stem: Evaluate \( \sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12}\right) \) using a sum or difference identity.
solution: Write \( \tfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12} \) as a sum/difference of special angles: \( \sin\!\left(\tfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12}\right)=[[answer]] \).

stem: Evaluate \( \cos\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12}\right) \) using a sum or difference identity.
solution: Write \( \tfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12} \) as a sum/difference of special angles: \( \cos\!\left(\tfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12}\right)=[[answer]] \).

stem: Evaluate \( \tan\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12}\right) \) using a sum or difference identity.
solution: Write \( \tfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12} \) as a sum/difference of special angles and apply \( \tan(\alpha\pm\beta)=\dfrac{\tan\alpha\pm\tan\beta}{1\mp\tan\alpha\tan\beta} \): \( \tan\!\left(\tfrac{[[k]]\pi}{12}\right)=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the reciprocal \( \cot \) of the correct value.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by \( +1 \).
This is off by \( -1 \).
This reports \( \sin \) instead of \( \tan \).
This reports \( \cos \) instead of \( \tan \).

stem: Suppose \( \sin\theta = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) and \( \theta \) is in Quadrant II. Use the double-angle identity to find \( \sin(2\theta) \).
solution: Since \( \theta \) is in Quadrant II, \( \cos\theta = -\sqrt{1-\sin^{2}\theta} = -\sqrt{1-\left(\tfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{2}} \). Then \( \sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign for \( \cos\theta \); cosine is negative in Quadrant II.
This squares \( \sin\theta \) instead of applying the double-angle formula.
This confuses the target with the cosine double-angle formula \( 1-2\sin^{2}\theta \).
This forgets the factor of 2 and a square root.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by a factor of 2.

stem: If \( \cos\theta = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \), use the double-angle identity \( \cos(2\theta)=2\cos^{2}\theta-1 \) to find \( \cos(2\theta) \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign, confusing this with \( 1-2\cos^{2}\theta \).
This forgets to square the cosine ratio.
This forgets the factor of 2.
This flips the sign of the trailing constant.
This is off by \( +1 \).
This has the wrong overall sign.

stem: If \( \sin\theta = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \), use the double-angle identity \( \cos(2\theta)=1-2\sin^{2}\theta \) to find \( \cos(2\theta) \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign, confusing this with \( 2\sin^{2}\theta-1 \).
This forgets to square the sine ratio.
This forgets the factor of 2 on the squared term.
This flips the sign of the leading constant.
This is off by \( -1 \).
This has the wrong overall sign.

stem: If \( \tan\theta = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \), use the double-angle identity \( \tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{2\tan\theta}{1-\tan^{2}\theta} \) to find \( \tan(2\theta) \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( 1+\tan^{2}\theta \) in the denominator instead of \( 1-\tan^{2}\theta \).
This forgets the factor of 2 in the numerator.
This forgets the denominator entirely.
This is the reciprocal, \( \cot(2\theta) \), of the correct value.
This is off by \( +1 \).
This has the wrong overall sign.

stem: If \( \cos(2\theta) = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \) and \( \theta \) is in Quadrant I, find \( \cos\theta \).
solution: Since \( \theta \) is in Quadrant I, \( \cos\theta>0 \). From \( \cos(2\theta)=2\cos^{2}\theta-1 \), \( \cos^{2}\theta=\dfrac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2} \), so \( \cos\theta=\sqrt{\dfrac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign; cosine is positive in Quadrant I.
This uses the sine half-angle formula \( \sqrt{\tfrac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2}} \) instead.
This stops at \( \cos^{2}\theta \) without taking the square root.
This is off by \( -1 \) from the correct value.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This is off by a factor of 2.

stem: If \( \cos(2\theta) = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \) and \( \theta \) is in Quadrant III, find \( \sin\theta \).
solution: Since \( \theta \) is in Quadrant III, \( \sin\theta<0 \). From \( \cos(2\theta)=1-2\sin^{2}\theta \), \( \sin^{2}\theta=\dfrac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2} \), so \( \sin\theta=-\sqrt{\dfrac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign; sine is negative in Quadrant III.
This uses the cosine half-angle formula \( \sqrt{\tfrac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}} \) instead.
This stops at \( \sin^{2}\theta \) without taking the square root.
This is off by \( -1 \) from the correct value.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This is off by a factor of 2.

stem: Let \( A \) and \( B \) be angles in Quadrant I with \( \sin A = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) and \( \cos B = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]} \). Find \( \sin(A+B) \).
solution: Since \( A,B \) are in Quadrant I, \( \cos A=\sqrt{1-\sin^{2}A}=\sqrt{1-\left(\tfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{2}} \) and \( \sin B=\sqrt{1-\cos^{2}B}=\sqrt{1-\left(\tfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]}\right)^{2}} \). By the sum identity, \( \sin(A+B)=\sin A\cos B+\cos A\sin B=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the difference identity \( \sin(A-B) \) instead of the sum identity.
This uses the cosine sum identity's pattern, \( \cos A\cos B-\sin A\sin B \), instead.
This adds the two given ratios directly instead of applying the sum identity.
This multiplies the two given ratios directly instead of applying the sum identity.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This is off by \( +1 \).

stem: Let \( A \) be an angle in Quadrant I with \( \cos A = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \), and let \( B \) be an angle in Quadrant II with \( \sin B = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]} \). Find \( \cos(A-B) \).
solution: Since \( A \) is in Quadrant I, \( \sin A=\sqrt{1-\cos^{2}A}=\sqrt{1-\left(\tfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{2}} \). Since \( B \) is in Quadrant II, \( \cos B=-\sqrt{1-\sin^{2}B}=-\sqrt{1-\left(\tfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]}\right)^{2}} \). By the difference identity, \( \cos(A-B)=\cos A\cos B+\sin A\sin B=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign for \( \cos B \); cosine is negative in Quadrant II.
This uses the sum identity \( \cos(A+B) \) instead of the difference identity.
This subtracts the two given ratios directly instead of applying the difference identity.
This multiplies the two given ratios directly instead of applying the difference identity.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This is off by \( +1 \).

stem: If \( \tan A = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) and \( \tan B = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]} \), use the tangent sum identity to find \( \tan(A+B) \).
solution: By the tangent sum identity, \( \tan(A+B)=\dfrac{\tan A+\tan B}{1-\tan A\tan B}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the tangent difference identity instead of the sum identity.
This uses the wrong sign in the denominator.
This forgets the denominator entirely.
This multiplies the two given ratios instead of applying the sum identity.
This is off by \( +1 \).
This has the wrong overall sign.
